text: The surgeon opens the gastrocolic omentum using a ligature device. The duodenum is identified and extensively mobilized using the Ligasure device, reaching the level of the aorta.

The surgeon identifies the infrapancreatic superior mesenteric vein, which gives rise to the right gastroepiploic vein. The vein is double-clipped and divided. The common hepatic artery is visible, with the gastroduodenal artery isolated, clipped, and divided. The portal vein and common bile duct are exposed lateral to the common hepatic artery.
The surgeon dissects the lymph node chain along the proper hepatic artery, extending the dissection to the level of the celiac axis, and removes the lymph nodes.

The surgeon identifies the cystic duct, which is double clipped and divided. The common hepatic duct is isolated using umbilical tape and divided above the cystic duct insertion. The distal end of the duct is secured with a vicryl endo loop, while the proximal end is controlled with a laparoscopic bulldog clamp.

The surgeon cross staples the antrum of the stomach using two firings of a linear laparoscopic stapler. An intraoperative ultrasound is performed to confirm that the mass is clear of adjacent vessels.
The surgeon creates a retropancreatic window and passes umbilical tape to guide the transection of the pancreatic neck, using a ligature device for the procedure.

The surgeon cross-staples and mobilizes the jejunum, delivering it to the right subhepatic space. The remaining attachments of the specimen to the superior mesenteric vein are carefully divided using a combination of ligature and hook cautery, allowing for the removal of the specimen.
The surgeon completes the hepaticojejunostomy using 3-0 Vicryl sutures on an SH needle, connecting the hepatic duct to the jejunum.

The surgeon constructs the pancreaticojejunostomy in two layers using 3-0 silk sutures on an RB needle, employing the duct mucosa technique. The pancreatic duct is clearly visible as the anastomosis begins.
The surgeon constructs the gastro-jejunostomy, closing the common channel with two firings of the laparoscopic linear stapler.
